channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2025 The New York Times Company

item.title: 3 Years Into Russia-Ukraine War, Trump Ushers in New World for Putin
item.description: Fresh possibilities have emerged for President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia with a change of power in Washington.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne
item.media:description: Ukrainian soldiers firing at Russian targets from the front line this month. President Vladimir V. Putin dug in on the battlefield to wait out Western resolve in a longer and more onerous conflict than Moscow had expected.

item.title: How Friedrich Merz Will Try to Lead Europe Despite a Weakened Hand
item.description: The likely next chancellor is unpopular, and his country’s economy has stalled. But his penchant for risks could help lead to a European showdown with President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley and Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:description: Friedrich Merz speaking in Berlin on Monday.

item.title: What Trump’s Deportation Plans Mean for Central America
item.description: Deporting migrants to Central American countries removes many political and legal hurdles for the Trump administration. How far can the practice go before these nations reach a breaking point?
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal
item.media:description: Migrants of various nationalities who were deported from the United States were taken to a remote shelter in Costa Rica on Friday.

item.title: Europe Prepares to Face Russia as Trump’s America Steps Back
item.description: European leaders visited Kyiv to mark the war’s third anniversary. The show of solidarity in Ukraine on Monday comes at a head-spinning moment for the continent.
item.dc:creator: Kim Barker, Jeanna Smialek and Steven Erlanger
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Ukrainian soldiers readied weapons and ammunition as they prepared to launch an assault on Russian soldiers in eastern Ukraine earlier this month.

item.title: Francis Is in Critical Condition. What Happens When a Pope Dies?
item.description: Time-honored rituals, drafted and refined over centuries, allow for ritual mourning and provide order at a dramatic moment of transition.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo and Jason Horowitz
item.media:credit: James Hill for The New York Times
item.media:description: Candles and flowers outside the Policlinico Agostino Gemelli, the hospital in Rome where Pope Francis is receiving treatment, on Monday.

item.title: As Trump Courts Putin, China’s Leader Xi Emphasizes Close Ties With Russia
item.description: The Chinese and Russian leaders reaffirmed their relationship in a video call on Monday, an apparent rebuff to the idea that the Trump administration could drive a wedge between them.
item.dc:creator: David Pierson and Paul Sonne
item.media:description: In a photo distributed by Russian state media, President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia holds a video call last month with President Xi Jinping of China.

item.title: Is Zelensky’s Approach to Trump Hurting Ukraine?
item.description: Through three years of wartime leadership, Ukraine’s president has mostly played weak hands wisely. But his approach has fallen flat with the Trump administration.
item.dc:creator: Andrew E. Kramer
item.category: Russian Interference in 2016 US Elections and Ties to Trump Associates
item.media:credit: James Hill for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Volodymyr Zelensky, right, with President Trump in Paris in December before his inauguration.

item.title: 5 Takeaways From Germany’s Election
item.description: Friedrich Merz and his party won, Elon Musk didn’t seem to move voters, and more lessons from an early German vote with big implications for Europe.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze and Jim Tankersley
item.media:credit: Sergey Ponomarev for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voting on Sunday at a polling station in Berlin. Turnout for the parliamentary election was the highest in years.

item.title: Hamas Official Expresses Reservations About Oct. 7 Attack on Israel
item.description: Mousa Abu Marzouk, the head of Hamas’s foreign relations office, said he would not have supported such an attack on Israel had he known of the devastation it would wreak on Gaza.
item.dc:creator: Adam Rasgon
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Hamas militants during the handover of three Israeli hostages to the Red Cross in Gaza on Saturday.

item.title: Gaza Cease-Fire Could End in Days, With No Extension Agreed. What Happens Next?
item.description: Less than a week before it expires, Israel and Hamas have failed to agree to extend the cease-fire in Gaza. Here’s how the deal could still continue — and what happens if it doesn’t.
item.dc:creator: Patrick Kingsley and Ronen Bergman
item.media:description: Palestinians waiting in Ramallah on Sunday after Israel delayed the release of Palestinian prisoners.

item.title: British Couple Arrested by Taliban in Afghanistan, Family Says
item.description: Peter and Barbie Reynolds were arrested on Feb. 1, their children told U.K. news outlets. The couple had run an education company in Afghanistan for almost two decades.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia

item.title: How TikTok Helped Germany’s Left to a Surprise Election Showing
item.description: Struggling a month ago, the Die Linke party surged into Parliament by riding a backlash against conservative immigration policy.
item.dc:creator: Tatiana Firsova and Jim Tankersley
item.media:description: Heidi Reichinnek, center, on Monday. Her party, Die Linke, surprised by winning nearly 9 percent of the vote in German elections on Sunday.

item.title: Ukraine Marks Third Anniversary of War With Little Fanfare
item.description: For Ukrainians, Feb. 24 was a day to remember, without major ceremony, what has been lost in the three years since Russia’s full-scale invasion.
item.dc:creator: Maria Varenikova
item.media:description: President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine and his wife, Olena Zelenska, visited Kyiv’s Maidan Square on Monday.

item.title: Ukraine Nears a Deal to Give U.S. a Share of Its Mineral Wealth
item.description: President Trump said that Ukraine’s president may soon visit the White House to sign a deal, though how it ties in to efforts to end the Russian invasion and guarantee Ukraine’s security is unclear.
item.dc:creator: Constant Méheut
item.media:description: In Kostiantynivka, an industrial city in eastern Ukraine, on Monday. The city has been under heavy bombardment from Russian forces.

item.title: Tuesday Briefing: The Ukraine War’s 3rd Anniversary
item.description: Plus, letting resorts run wild.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.media:description: Bomb damage in Kostiantynivka, Ukraine, on Monday.

item.title: Trump, Meeting With Macron, Says He Might Visit Russia
item.description: Meeting at the White House, President Trump declined to call President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia a dictator while President Emmanuel Macron of France stated flatly that “the aggressor is Russia.”
item.dc:creator: Peter Baker
item.media:description: President Donald J. Trump with President Emmanuel Macron of France, in the Oval Office on Monday.

item.title: Why Tensions Are Rising Over Israel’s Operations in the West Bank
item.description: Amid the most intensive Israeli operations in the area in decades, Palestinians fear entrenched displacement and a return to military control in some towns.
item.dc:creator: Fatima AbdulKarim and Erika Solomon
item.media:description: Israeli soldiers during a military operation inside the Jenin refugee camp on Monday.

item.title: U.S. Votes Against U.N. Resolution Demanding Russian Withdrawal from Ukraine
item.description: The United States voted against a resolution, backed by most of Europe, demanding Russian withdrawal from Ukraine — a deep fissure between allies, and a sharp shift in American policy.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:description: Mariana Betsa, deputy foreign minister of Ukraine, addresses the U.N. General Assembly, urging approval of a resolution demanding that Russia withdraw forces from Ukraine, in New York on Monday.

item.title: Elon Musk Congratulates AfD Leader on Germany’s Election Results
item.description: The leader of the party said she received the call after the party known as the AfD finished second in national elections, by far the best showing in its history.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:description: Alice Weidel, leader of the Alternative for Germany party, talking via video link with Elon Musk at an election rally in Halle, Germany, last month.

item.title: Hopes Fade for Workers Trapped for Days in Tunnel in India
item.description: Local officials have been unable to make contact with eight people after wall of silt and water caused the ceiling to collapse.
item.dc:creator: Ali Watkins
item.media:description: Search teams are working to rescue eight workers believed to be trapped in a tunnel that collapsed in Indian state of Telangana.

item.title: Frank G. Wisner, Diplomat With Impact on Foreign Policy, Dies at 86
item.description: He headed U.S. embassies around the world and relished the role, bringing a gregarious style to promoting American interests. But he clashed with the Obama White House.
item.dc:creator: Alan Cowell
item.media:description: Frank G. Wisner II in 2007. The guest list at his dinner parties offered a Who’s Who of the elite.

item.title: In Russia, Anniversary of Ukraine War Draws Little Public Mention
item.description: President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia is not scheduled to attend any events on Monday to mark the anniversary of the full-scale invasion that he ordered.
item.dc:creator: Nataliya Vasilyeva
item.media:description: Red Square in Moscow was open to visitors rather than filled with commemorations on Monday.

item.title: Trial of Former Surgeon Accused of Abusing Nearly 300 Opens in France
item.description: A former surgeon is accused of raping or sexually assaulting 299 people, mostly children, over 25 years. It is considered to be France’s largest-ever pedophilia case.
item.dc:creator: Aurelien Breeden
item.media:description: The trial of retired surgeon Joël Le Scouarnec, who is accused of raping or sexually assaulting 299 people over 25 years, started on Monday in Vannes, in Brittany.

item.title: How Denmark’s Social Democrats Are Succeeding With Stricter Immigration Policies
item.description: Around the world, progressive parties have come to see tight immigration restrictions as unnecessary, even cruel. What if they’re actually the only way for progressivism to flourish?
item.dc:creator: David Leonhardt

item.title: 5 Photos Show How the Ukraine War Changed
item.description: On the third anniversary of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, Tyler Hicks, a war photographer for The New York Times, looks back at five photos he took during the conflict and reflects on how the rules of engagement have changed, affecting what he can document and what the world can witness.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Hicks, Christina Thornell, Rebecca Suner, Gaëlle Girbes and David Jouppi

item.title: Pope Francis, in Critical Condition, Is ‘Resting’ in Hospital, Vatican Says
item.description: Francis is being treated for pneumonia, a complex infection and kidney problems that have left him in critical condition. He did some light work on Monday.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola and Gina Kolata
item.media:credit: James Hill for The New York Times
item.media:description: Flowers, candles and balloons have been left at the feet of a statue of former Pope John Paul II at the hospital in Rome where Pope Francis is being treated.

item.title: Every Planet in Our Solar System Will Be Visible at Night This Week
item.description: With Mercury joining the show, all seven of Earth’s celestial neighbors will be present at dusk this week.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller

item.title: Greenpeace Goes to Court in $300 Million Suit That Poses Bankruptcy Risk
item.description: In a closely watched case, the owner of the Dakota Access Pipeline is claiming the environmental group masterminded protests that hurt the company’s business.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Alyssa Schukar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Protesters at the Dakota Access Pipeline in 2016.

item.title: North Koreans on Chinese Tuna Boats Boost Revenue for Kim Jong-un
item.description: Thousands of miles from home, North Koreans work on Chinese tuna longliners in the Indian Ocean for pay that goes to their leader, a new study says.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun and Muktita Suhartono

item.title: Monday Briefing
item.description: Results from Germany’s election.
item.dc:creator: Natasha Frost
item.media:description: Friedrich Merz has promised to crack down on immigration and to cut taxes and business regulations.

item.title: In One Woman’s Life, the Story of Ukraine’s War, 3 Years On
item.description: In treatment for cancer and with her husband in a Russian prison, Olha Kurtmallaieva, 25, worries that time may be running out for her and, possibly, her country.
item.dc:creator: Kim Barker
item.media:credit: Oksana Parafeniuk for The New York Times
item.media:description: Olha Kurtmallaieva at home on Friday in Kyiv, Ukraine. She was diagnosed with cancer in 2021, about six months before the Russians invaded.

item.title: Kenyan Police Officer Deployed to Tackle Gangs in Haiti Killed
item.description: The officer’s death appeared to be the first among the Kenyan forces that were sent to Haiti last year as part of an international effort to subdue gang violence.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang and Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.media:description: Kenyan police officers in Port-au-Prince, Haiti, earlier this month. Hundreds of Kenyan police officers have been deployed to Haiti to reinforce a security mission to tackle gang violence.

item.title: German Election 2025: Friedrich Merz Appears Poised to Be Next Chancellor
item.description: The Christian Democrat, who appeared to have a path to a stable governing coalition, hopes to lead Europe’s response to President Trump’s reshuffling of alliances.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.media:description: Friedrich Merz, the leader of Germany’s Christian Democrats, said his top priority as chancellor would be “to strengthen Europe as quickly as possible.”

item.title: Germany Election Votes Are Cast. What Happens Now?
item.description: The country appears headed for a coalition government. But the process of building one could take months.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:credit: Katrin Streicher for The New York Times
item.media:description: Election posters in Lutherstadt Wittenberg, Germany, last week.

item.title: Monday Briefing: Conservatives Near Victory in Germany
item.description: Plus, a pharaoh’s tomb was unearthed.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.media:description: Friedrich Merz, leader of Germany’s Christian Democratic Union.

item.title: Why the Far-Right AfD Is Unlikely to Join Germany’s Next Government
item.description: The collective shunning of the AfD party is part of a longstanding attempt to blunt its move into the mainstream.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.media:credit: Sergey Ponomarev for The New York Times
item.media:description: An AfD campaign event in Halle, Germany, in January.

item.title: Who Is Friedrich Merz, the Man Poised to Be Germany’s Next Chancellor?
item.description: Friedrich Merz, head of the conservative Christian Democrats, has vowed to bring stronger leadership to Europe, where the new Trump administration has sowed anxiety.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger and Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:description: Friedrich Merz, the leader of the Christian Democratic Union, this month in Berlin.

item.title: Expanding West Bank Campaign, Israel Sends Tanks for First Time in Decades
item.description: Israel’s defense minister, Israel Katz, said that 40,000 Palestinian residents displaced from militant hotbeds would not be allowed to return to their homes.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner
item.media:description: Israeli tanks entering the Jenin camp in the occupied West Bank on Sunday.

item.title: German Voters Took Note of Election Interference by Trump Administration Officials
item.description: Several voters took note of a speech by Vice President JD Vance this month, when he told European leaders to stop shunning parties deemed “extreme.”
item.dc:creator: Catherine Odom and Sam Gurwitt
item.media:description: Vice President JD Vance at the Munich Security Conference this month.

item.title: Here are the latest developments.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley, Christopher F. Schuetze and Melissa Eddy

item.title: Amid Regional Diplomatic Furor, Sudan’s Paramilitaries Forge a Rival Government
item.description: The Rapid Support Forces said it was paving the way to an end to the civil war. Critics called it an audacious gambit by a group that the United States has accused of genocide.
item.dc:creator: Declan Walsh
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:description: A damaged building in Wad Madani, Sudan, that was retaken by the Sudanese Army from its rival Rapid Support Forces this month.

item.title: Israel and Hamas Trade Accusations of Violating Fragile Cease-Fire
item.description: Hamas criticized Israel’s decision to delay the release of hundreds of Palestinian prisoners, amid growing tensions and concerns for the future of the truce in Gaza.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Hamas handing over three Israeli hostages on Saturday to the Red Cross in Nuseirat, in the Gaza Strip.

item.title: Zelensky Pushes Back Against U.S. Mineral Deal and Announces European Summit
item.description: The Ukrainian leader also said he would step down if Ukraine was allowed to join NATO, though it was unclear if he was serious or not.
item.dc:creator: Constant Méheut and Andrew E. Kramer
item.media:description: President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine has traded blows with President Trump amid an escalating feud over peace talks.

item.title: This Christian Convert Fled Iran, and Ran Into Trump’s Deportation Policy
item.description: Artemis Ghasemzadeh is among a group of migrants being held in a jungle camp in Panama.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi and Hamed Aleaziz

item.title: No Exit
item.description: About 300 migrants from all around the world were deported from the United States to Panama. There, they were trapped in a hotel, facing a choice of whether to return to places they had fled.
item.dc:creator: Federico Rios
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times

item.title: Pope Francis Suffering From Kidney Failure in Addition to Pneumonia
item.description: The kidney ailment appears to be in an early stage, the Vatican said, adding that the pontiff is still receiving high flows of oxygen for his respiratory illness.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo
item.media:description: Deacons arriving at the Vatican on Sunday for a Mass that was supposed to be celebrated by Pope Francis.

item.title: Catholics Pray for Ailing Pope Francis at Sunday Mass
item.description: The 88-year-old pontiff, who has been hospitalized for more than a week, had a restful night after receiving oxygen and a blood transfusion on Saturday, the Vatican said.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang and Camille Elemia
item.media:description: Sunday Mass in St. Peter’s Basilica at the Vatican, a service that Pope Francis had intended to preside over. He has been hospitalized for more than a week.

item.title: Macron and Starmer Have Played Trump’s Game Before, but the Rules Are Changing
item.description: The leaders of France and Britain are both due to meet with President Trump this week. Approaches previously tried with him may no longer work.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:description: President Emmanuel Macron of France, left, and Prime Minister Keir Starmer of Britain will have to navigate shifting alliances as they meet with President Trump in Washington.

item.title: Germany Election 2025: What to Watch For
item.description: What Germans decide in national elections that are likely to produce a new chancellor will be of critical importance as Europe re-evaluates relations with the Trump administration.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:description: Campaign posters in Berlin last month.

item.title: Hezbollah Stages Show of Strength at Hassan Nasrallah’s Funeral
item.description: The Iran-backed Lebanese militant group held an elaborate commemoration for its longtime leader, Hassan Nasrallah, who was killed by Israel in September.
item.dc:creator: Christina Goldbaum and Euan Ward
item.media:credit: Diego Ibarra Sanchez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Hezbollah supporters gathered on Sunday to pay tribute to Hassan Nasrallah, the group’s former leader, and Hashem Saffiedine, another top official, on Sunday outside Beirut, Lebanon.

item.title: The Battle of Iwo Jima: A Photo History
item.description: U.S. Marines raised an American flag on the Japanese island’s highest peak exactly 80 years ago. But the fighting, some of the deadliest of World War II, would rage on for a month.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Beech
item.media:description: U.S. Marines of the 28th Regiment of the Fifth Division planted an American flag atop Mount Suribachi, Iwo Jima, on Feb. 23, 1945.

item.title: Hamas Frees 6 Hostages as Israel Delays Palestinian Prisoner Release
item.description: The six Israelis were the last living captives set to be released in the first phase of the cease-fire. The agreement, strained for weeks, has an uncertain future.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner, Aaron Boxerman, Adam Rasgon and Fatima AbdulKarim
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: From left to right, Eliya Cohen, Omer Shem Tov, and Omer Wenkert, three of the six hostages who were released on Saturday, at an orchestrated ceremony in Nuseirat, Gaza.

item.title: ‘Religious Motivation’ Possible in Berlin Stabbing, Police Say
item.description: The suspect, a Syrian refugee, told the police that a plan had come into his mind to kill Jews. The attack raises tensions just before an election in which immigration is a big issue.
item.dc:creator: Eve Sampson
item.media:description: The Holocaust Memorial in Berlin was cordoned off by the police as a crime scene on Saturday.

item.title: Trump Appointees Fire Hundreds at U.S.A.I.D. Working on Urgent Aid
item.description: The firings added to doubts about whether Secretary of State Marco Rubio, the agency’s acting head, supports lifesaving humanitarian assistance, as he has said he does.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong and Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.category: United States Agency for International Development
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Trump administration appointees running the United States Agency for International Development have fired hundreds of employees in recent days.

item.title: Nine-Month Manhunt for French Fugitive Finally Produces an Arrest
item.description: A French convict was arrested in Romania months after he had been freed in a brazen daytime ambush that killed two prison guards, authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Eve Sampson and Liz Alderman
item.media:description: The Central Arrest of Bucharest, where French convict and drug trafficker Mohamed Amra was detained after his arrest, on Saturday.

item.title: ‘Terrorist’ Attack at Market in France Leaves One Dead
item.description: The suspect, an Algerian national who was on France’s terrorist watch list, was arrested.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.media:description: Police officers collected evidence at the site of a knife attack in Mulhouse, in eastern France, on Saturday.

item.title: Pope Francis in Critical Condition After ‘Respiratory Crisis,’ Vatican Says
item.description: The announcement on Saturday evening indicated that Francis’ medical condition had become more complicated since an update from his doctors on Friday.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo
item.media:description: The hospital where Pope Francis is undergoing treatment in Rome.
